text: Single-pixel imaging has attracted increasing attention in the last few years for imaging around obstacles or through opaque media. However, achieving a high image quality is a significant challenge, especially in the presence of strong background noise. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective single-pixel imaging method to obtain high-quality images of a scene by sequentially obtaining the Fourier transform of the image of the scene. To achieve this, we use four-step phase-shifting sinusoidal structured illumination to record the desired spatial frequencies. An inverse Fourier transform is then applied to the spectrum to provide the image of the scene. Unlike existing single-pixel imaging approaches, our method only relies on the integrated intensity of the collected light and not on the spatial resolution provided by the detector, thus allowing flexibility in the position of the sensor and allowing the detector to be located around corners or behind diffusers and other opaque media. In addition, the proposed method takes advantage of the fact that most natural images have a sparse Fourier transform, which allows for high-quality image reconstruction using sub-Nyquist measurements. Several experiments have been carried out to validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, demonstrating that the proposed approach significantly reduces the impact of background noise and eliminates the DC component of the image, allowing high-quality images to be obtained. This is demonstrated with other single-pixel imaging methods that are unable to obtain a useful image in strong background noise environments such as the white LED lighting background.
